threats.title: Epoch Boundary Miscalculation in update_market Applies Wrong Reward Rates Across Epoch Boundaries
threats.hypothesis_description: The update_market loop calculates `nextEpoch = i + BLOCK_EPOCH` instead of `((i / BLOCK_EPOCH) + 1) * BLOCK_EPOCH`. When `lastRewardBlock` is not epoch-aligned (the common case), the loop fails to split reward calculations at actual epoch boundaries. Blocks belonging to epoch N+1 are rewarded using epoch N's `cantoPerBlock` rate and gauge weight, systematically misallocating rewards across all markets.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a structural logic error in the core reward distribution algorithm. Every update_market call spanning an epoch boundary produces incorrect accCantoPerShare. If epoch rates differ, users receive more or fewer rewards than entitled. An attacker can strategically time update_market calls to benefit from using a higher-rate epoch's cantoPerBlock for blocks that belong to a lower-rate epoch.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `uint256 nextEpoch = i + BLOCK_EPOCH` instead of `epoch + BLOCK_EPOCH`. When i=150000 and BLOCK_EPOCH=100000, epoch=100000 but nextEpoch=250000, overshooting the true next boundary at 200000.

preconditions: Market is whitelisted
lastRewardBlock is not epoch-aligned (very common after any mid-epoch update)
cantoPerBlock differs between consecutive epochs

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: nextEpoch = i + BLOCK_EPOCH does not advance to the actual epoch boundary
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Blocks in epoch N+1 get epoch N's cantoPerBlock rate and gauge weight. Example: lastRewardBlock=150000, epoch=100000, nextEpoch calculated as 250000, so blocks 200000-249999 use cantoPerBlock[100000] instead of cantoPerBlock[200000].

analysis_questions: What is the quantifiable reward difference per epoch boundary crossing?
Can an attacker strategically trigger update_market at specific blocks to maximize benefit?
Does the gauge_relative_weight also use the wrong epoch, compounding the error?
validation_requirements: Trace the loop with concrete values across 3+ epoch boundaries
Compare calculated accCantoPerShare against correct epoch-aligned calculation
Quantify cumulative reward error over extended periods

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Governance sets cantoPerBlock[epoch_A]=10e18 and cantoPerBlock[epoch_B]=1e18. An attacker ensures update_market runs at a block where lastRewardBlock is mid-epoch_A, causing epoch_A's higher rate to apply to blocks in epoch_B. Over many blocks, the attacker earns 10x the intended rewards for those misattributed blocks.
prerequisites: Different cantoPerBlock rates across consecutive epochs

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Systematic over/under-distribution of CANTO rewards across all markets. If rates change significantly between epochs, the misallocation can be proportional to the rate difference multiplied by the number of blocks in the wrong epoch.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users in all markets where updates span epoch boundaries
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade; accumulated accounting errors are permanent

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for reward correctness across epoch boundaries with varying rates

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: No capital needed - exploitable by any user
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time rates differ across epochs
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing on-chain rewards against expected epoch-correct values

threats.title: Silent uint128 Truncation of Reward Accumulators Corrupts Accounting and Breaks Monotonicity
threats.hypothesis_description: Both `accCantoPerShare` and `secRewardsPerShare` are stored as uint128 but computed in uint256. In Solidity 0.8.17, explicit narrowing casts like `uint128(value)` silently truncate (higher-order bits are cut off) without reverting. When `marketSupply` is very small (e.g., 1 wei) and rewards are active, `(cantoReward * 1e18) / marketSupply` can exceed uint128.max (~3.4e38), wrapping accCantoPerShare to a dramatically lower value and destroying the monotonicity invariant.
threats.investigation_rationale: With marketSupply=1 and cantoPerBlock=1e18, the accumulator grows by ~5e35 per block. It overflows uint128 in ~680 blocks (~22 minutes). This permanently corrupts reward accounting for the market: existing users lose accumulated unclaimed rewards, and future reward calculations are based on a corrupted base value.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `market.accCantoPerShare += uint128((cantoReward * 1e18) / marketSupply)` and `market.secRewardsPerShare += uint128((blockDelta * 1e18) / marketSupply)` - explicit uint128 cast silently truncates overflow

preconditions: Market is whitelisted with nonzero cantoPerBlock and nonzero gauge weight
lendingMarketTotalBalance is very small (1 wei achievable by attacker withdrawing all but dust)
Enough blocks pass between updates for the accumulator to overflow uint128

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: uint128() explicit cast on uint256 arithmetic result
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: With marketSupply=1, cantoPerBlock=1e18, weight=5e17: per-block increment = 5e17 * 1e18 / 1 = 5e35. uint128.max ≈ 3.4e38. Overflow in ~680 blocks.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No overflow check before cast, no minimum market supply requirement

analysis_questions: Can an attacker controllably reduce marketSupply to 1 wei by withdrawing from the lending market?
After truncation/wrap, what is the exact impact on existing users' claimable rewards?
Can the attacker profit directly from the truncation or only cause griefing?
validation_requirements: Calculate exact overflow block count for realistic parameter ranges
Simulate the truncation and trace its effect on claim() for existing users
Verify that Solidity 0.8.17 uint128() cast truncates (not reverts)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deposits 1 wei into a whitelisted market, then withdraws all other liquidity. With nonzero rewards, accCantoPerShare grows exponentially fast. After ~680 blocks, it overflows uint128, wrapping to a small value. All other users' unclaimed rewards are destroyed. The attacker claims just before overflow to capture rewards, then lets the accumulator corrupt.
prerequisites: Ability to deposit/withdraw through a whitelisted lending market
Low liquidity market or ability to drain liquidity

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete loss of accumulated unclaimed rewards for all users in the affected market
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors in the affected market
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade or market migration; accumulated accounting is permanently corrupted

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for accumulator overflow with low market supply

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: ~680 blocks wait time with aggressive parameters
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring accCantoPerShare for non-monotonic behavior

threats.title: Incomplete Secondary Rewards Implementation with TODO Marker Produces Incorrect Accounting
threats.hypothesis_description: The `secRewardsPerShare` calculation contains a `TODO: Scaling` comment and computes `(blockDelta * 1e18) / marketSupply` without any actual secondary reward rate. This tracks raw "blocks per share" rather than scaled reward amounts. Third-party platforms relying on this field for secondary reward distribution will produce incorrect calculations, especially for tokens with non-18 decimals.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code explicitly acknowledges incomplete implementation via the TODO comment. Since third-party lending platforms are documented as reading this state to distribute secondary rewards, incorrect values directly cause misallocation of secondary rewards. The `secRewardDebt` field in sync_ledger also uses this unscaled value.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `market.secRewardsPerShare += uint128((blockDelta * 1e18) / marketSupply); // TODO: Scaling` - explicit TODO comment indicating incomplete implementation

preconditions: Third-party platforms consume secRewardsPerShare and secRewardDebt for reward calculations
Market has active deposits

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: TODO comment with no actual reward rate applied to secondary rewards
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: secRewardsPerShare tracks blocks/share, not actual secondary reward amounts. No configurable secondary reward rate exists. Third parties cannot correctly derive reward amounts from this value without external context.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No secondary reward rate configuration, no scaling factor

analysis_questions: How do third-party platforms interpret secRewardsPerShare?
Does the lack of scaling cause over/under-distribution of secondary rewards?
Is secRewardDebt correctly maintained if secRewardsPerShare is unscaled?
validation_requirements: Review third-party integration documentation for expected secRewardsPerShare format
Verify secRewardDebt consistency across deposit/withdrawal cycles
Assess impact of missing scaling on actual reward distributions

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Third-party platform reads secRewardsPerShare as scaled reward amounts and distributes secondary tokens proportionally. Due to missing scaling, the distributed amounts are orders of magnitude wrong, either draining secondary reward pools or underpaying users.
prerequisites: Third-party platforms actively using secRewardsPerShare for reward distribution

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Incorrect secondary reward distribution by all integrated third-party platforms
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users expecting secondary rewards from integrated lending platforms
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to add proper scaling

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for secondary reward accuracy or third-party integration

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing expected vs actual secondary reward distributions

threats.title: Reentrancy in update_market Loop via GaugeController External Call Allows Double-Counting of Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: The `update_market` function modifies `accCantoPerShare` incrementally inside a loop but only sets `lastRewardBlock = block.number` AFTER the loop completes. Inside each loop iteration, an external call to `gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write()` occurs. If this external call triggers a callback into LendingLedger (directly or indirectly), a re-entrant call to update_market for the same market would find `lastRewardBlock` still at its old value and re-run the entire loop, double-counting all reward blocks.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `_write` suffix on gauge_relative_weight_write implies state mutation in GaugeController. If GaugeController has any callback mechanism, hooks, or if it's upgradeable, a re-entrant path exists. The code has no reentrancy guard. The state (accCantoPerShare) is modified before the protective state (lastRewardBlock) is updated, violating CEI within the loop context.
deployment_assumptions: GaugeController implementation does not trigger callbacks to LendingLedger during gauge_relative_weight_write
threats.config_evidence: GaugeController is set at construction (LendingLedger.sol line 40) and cannot be changed. No GaugeController source provided to verify callback safety.

proof_of_signal.pattern: accCantoPerShare modified inside loop (line 66) with external call to gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write (line 64) BEFORE lastRewardBlock is set (line 71). Loop variable `i` is local and unaffected by nested calls.

preconditions: GaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write() must trigger a callback path into LendingLedger
Market must be whitelisted
There must be blocks to process (block.number > lastRewardBlock)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: State modification (accCantoPerShare +=) inside loop with external call, protective state (lastRewardBlock) set only after loop
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Re-entrant update_market sees stale lastRewardBlock and re-processes all blocks, doubling accCantoPerShare. The outer loop continues after the callback, adding even more.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No reentrancy guard (nonReentrant modifier), no in-loop update of lastRewardBlock

analysis_questions: Does GaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write() have any callback or hook mechanism?
Is GaugeController upgradeable or does it delegate to other contracts?
Can the GaugeController implementation trigger any external call that loops back?
validation_requirements: Review GaugeController source code for callback paths
Trace complete execution of gauge_relative_weight_write for re-entrancy vectors
Simulate re-entrant update_market and quantify accCantoPerShare inflation

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If GaugeController re-enters update_market during gauge_relative_weight_write, accCantoPerShare is inflated by 2x or more. The attacker then claims the inflated rewards, draining the contract's CANTO balance. Even without malicious intent, an upgraded or compromised GaugeController could trigger this inadvertently.
prerequisites: GaugeController with callback mechanism or upgradeability

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: 2x or more inflation of accCantoPerShare leading to complete drainage of contract CANTO balance
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users - early claimers drain funds, later claimers get nothing
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade and manual accounting correction

threats.test_coverage_gap: No reentrancy test for update_market via GaugeController callback

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: None - any user with deposits can trigger
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring accCantoPerShare for abnormal jumps

threats.title: Gas DoS on Stale Markets Due to Unbounded update_market Loop with External Calls
threats.hypothesis_description: The `update_market` function loops from `lastRewardBlock` to `block.number` in epoch-sized chunks. Each iteration makes an external call to `gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write()` which modifies storage (implied by `_write` suffix). If a market goes unupdated for many epochs, the accumulated iterations with storage-writing external calls can exceed the block gas limit, permanently bricking all operations (claim, sync_ledger) for that market.
threats.investigation_rationale: Each loop iteration involves at minimum: one external call with state write (~20-50K gas), storage reads, uint128 storage writes, and arithmetic. At ~50K gas per iteration and a 30M gas block limit, ~600 iterations is the maximum. With BLOCK_EPOCH=100,000 blocks (~1 week), 600 weeks (~11 years) of inactivity would be needed. However, with the epoch boundary bug (TECH-H001), if iterations are smaller, fewer epochs could trigger this. Additionally, GaugeController may use more gas than estimated.

proof_of_signal.pattern: while loop from lastRewardBlock to block.number with external call gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write inside each iteration, no gas check or batch limit

preconditions: Market is whitelisted but inactive for an extended period
No intermediate update_market calls to advance lastRewardBlock
gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write consumes significant gas per call

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unbounded while loop with external call, no mechanism for incremental catch-up
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No upper bound on loop iterations; no function to partially advance lastRewardBlock
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No maximum iteration limit, no partial update mechanism

analysis_questions: What is the actual gas cost per loop iteration including the GaugeController call?
How many iterations are needed to exceed the block gas limit on Canto?
Is there any way to partially advance lastRewardBlock without completing the full loop?
validation_requirements: Benchmark gas per iteration with real GaugeController
Calculate maximum safe staleness period
Test update_market with progressively larger block gaps

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A market is whitelisted but sees no activity for months or years. When a user finally tries to claim or the market tries to sync, update_market must process all missed blocks. With high gas per iteration, the transaction runs out of gas. All operations are permanently blocked for this market.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent lockout of all user rewards and market operations for the affected market
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users with deposits in the stale market
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: No recovery path in current contract; requires upgrade or migration

threats.test_coverage_gap: No gas limit test for update_market with large block gaps

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Requires extended period of market inactivity

threats.title: No Solvency Check Allows Reward Claims to Drain Contract Beyond Available Balance
threats.hypothesis_description: The `claim()` function sends CANTO via `msg.sender.call{value: uint256(cantoToSend)}` without checking that the contract holds sufficient balance. The `setRewards()` function sets arbitrary reward rates without verifying funded CANTO. Early claimers can drain all available CANTO, leaving later claimers permanently unable to claim despite having valid accumulated rewards. While the transaction would revert on insufficient balance (protecting state), a race develops where the first claimers get full rewards and later claimers get nothing.
threats.investigation_rationale: There is no enforcement that governance deposits enough CANTO to cover configured rewards. The contract tracks liabilities (accCantoPerShare) that grow independently of assets (contract balance). This creates a fundamental accounting gap where obligations can exceed assets.

proof_of_signal.pattern: CANTO sent without balance check: `msg.sender.call{value: uint256(cantoToSend)}`. setRewards at lines 109-113 sets arbitrary rates without funding verification.

preconditions: Reward rates configured via setRewards
Contract CANTO balance less than total accumulated pending rewards
Multiple users with claimable rewards

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No balance >= cantoToSend check before transfer
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: rewardDebt is updated BEFORE the transfer (line 96), but the entire transaction reverts if transfer fails. So state is preserved, but claimers who arrive after balance is drained can never claim.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No solvency tracking, no balance check, no deposit-matched reward configuration

analysis_questions: What happens when contract balance runs out mid-claim?
Is the revert atomic enough to protect all state changes?
Can governance recover by sending more CANTO later?
validation_requirements: Trace claim execution when contract balance < cantoToSend
Verify atomicity of revert across all state changes
Model insolvency scenarios with multiple markets and users

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Governance sets high cantoPerBlock but only funds the contract partially. As rewards accumulate across multiple markets, early claimers drain available CANTO. Remaining users' claim() calls revert on the transfer, creating a first-come-first-served race for limited funds.
prerequisites: Contract underfunded relative to configured reward rates

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Later claimers permanently unable to access earned rewards
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users who claim after the contract is drained
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Governance can send more CANTO to the contract address

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for claim behavior under insufficient contract balance

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Ongoing risk from deployment if underfunded
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing contract balance against total pending claims

threats.title: Re-Whitelisting Market Creates Stale accCantoPerShare Without Resetting User Positions
threats.hypothesis_description: When a market is re-whitelisted, `lastRewardBlock` is reset to `block.number` but `accCantoPerShare` retains its old value. Users who had deposits before de-listing still have nonzero `user.amount` and old `rewardDebt` values. The lending market could not call `sync_ledger` during the de-listing period, so if users withdrew from the actual lending market during that period, `lendingMarketTotalBalance` and `user.amount` in LendingLedger are now stale and inconsistent with reality.
threats.investigation_rationale: After re-whitelisting, the user.amount in LendingLedger no longer reflects actual lending market positions. New rewards accrue based on stale, inflated total balances, diluting legitimate depositors. Users who withdrew from the actual lending market during de-listing still earn rewards post re-listing based on phantom balances.

proof_of_signal.pattern: whiteListLendingMarket only updates lastRewardBlock when re-whitelisting; accCantoPerShare, lendingMarketTotalBalance, and all userInfo entries remain unchanged

preconditions: Market was whitelisted with active users
Governance de-whitelists and later re-whitelists the market
Users interacted with the actual lending market during the de-listing period

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Only lastRewardBlock is reset on re-whitelist; all user accounting preserved
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: sync_ledger reverts during de-listing, so lending market cannot update LendingLedger state. Post re-whitelist, LendingLedger state diverges from actual market positions.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No state reconciliation mechanism on re-whitelist, no forced sync

analysis_questions: What happens if a lending market catches sync_ledger reverts and processes user withdrawals anyway?
Can phantom balances earn and claim rewards post re-whitelisting?
Is there any mechanism for the lending market to reconcile state after re-whitelisting?
validation_requirements: Simulate de-whitelist → user withdraws from market → re-whitelist → user claims
Verify whether phantom balances accrue rewards
Check if lending market implementation handles sync_ledger reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Market is de-whitelisted. User withdraws cNOTE from the actual lending market (sync_ledger reverts, ignored). Market is re-whitelisted. LendingLedger still shows user with large balance. New rewards accrue and user claims CANTO based on phantom balance they no longer have.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users can claim rewards based on phantom balances they no longer hold
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Legitimate current depositors whose rewards are diluted by phantom positions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Lending market must manually reconcile all user positions after re-whitelisting

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for de-whitelist → re-whitelist state consistency

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing LendingLedger balances with actual market balances

threats.title: GaugeController Revert in update_market Loop Permanently DoS Markets
threats.hypothesis_description: The `update_market` function calls `gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write(_market, epoch)` inside a loop. If GaugeController reverts for any epoch (e.g., market not registered in GaugeController, invalid epoch, or GaugeController paused/upgraded), the entire `update_market` call reverts. Since `update_market` is called by both `claim()` and `sync_ledger()`, a persistent GaugeController revert permanently blocks all operations for the affected market.
threats.investigation_rationale: There's no try-catch around the GaugeController call, and no fallback behavior if the call fails. A mismatch between LendingLedger's whitelist and GaugeController's registered gauges would create a permanent DoS. As lastRewardBlock falls further behind, the problem compounds (more iterations, more potential revert points).
deployment_assumptions: All whitelisted markets must also be properly registered in GaugeController for all relevant epochs
threats.config_evidence: GaugeController set at constructor (line 40). No verification in whiteListLendingMarket that the market exists in GaugeController. No GaugeController source provided.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write(_market, epoch)` called without try-catch; revert propagates to all callers

preconditions: Market whitelisted in LendingLedger but not properly registered in GaugeController
OR GaugeController upgraded/paused/broken for specific epoch
Market has marketSupply > 0

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: External call without try-catch in a critical path
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No fallback if GaugeController reverts; all dependent functions permanently blocked
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No try-catch, no default weight fallback, no whitelist-to-gauge validation

analysis_questions: Under what conditions does gauge_relative_weight_write revert?
Is there a validation path ensuring market registration in both systems?
Can governance recover from this state?
validation_requirements: Review GaugeController for revert conditions in gauge_relative_weight_write
Test whitelisting a market not registered in GaugeController
Verify if governance can de-whitelist and re-whitelist to unstick

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Governance whitelists market A in LendingLedger but forgets to add gauge in GaugeController. Users deposit and lending market calls sync_ledger. update_market tries to call GaugeController which reverts. All operations for market A are permanently blocked. Users' deposits in the lending market may also be blocked if the market requires successful sync_ledger.
prerequisites: Misconfiguration between LendingLedger whitelist and GaugeController registrations

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete DoS for affected market, users cannot claim rewards or sync balances
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of the misconfigured market
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: May be recoverable by registering the market in GaugeController, depending on its implementation

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for GaugeController revert handling in update_market

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable immediately when any operation is attempted

threats.title: De-Whitelisting Market Permanently Locks Accrued User Rewards with No Emergency Claim Path
threats.hypothesis_description: When governance de-whitelists a market via `whiteListLendingMarket(_market, false)`, the `update_market` function reverts with 'Market not whitelisted'. Since both `claim()` and `sync_ledger()` call `update_market` first, ALL operations for the market are permanently blocked. Users with accrued but unclaimed CANTO rewards lose access with no emergency withdrawal or grace period mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a design-level deficiency where governance has the power to irreversibly lock user funds. While documented trust assumptions say governance is non-malicious, there is no grace period, emergency claim function, or user notification mechanism. Even accidental de-whitelisting with immediate re-whitelisting resets lastRewardBlock, potentially skipping reward periods.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Line 56: `require(lendingMarketWhitelist[_market])` blocks all operations. Line 117-122: de-whitelisting provides no grace period. No emergency claim function exists.

preconditions: Governance de-whitelists a market with active users having unclaimed rewards
No emergency claim mechanism exists

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Whitelist check gates all operations with no bypass for existing claims
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: De-whitelisting instantly blocks claim() and sync_ledger(). If lending market depends on sync_ledger for user withdrawals, user deposits in the actual market may also be stuck.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No emergency claim function, no grace period, no state preservation on de-whitelist

analysis_questions: Can governance re-whitelist to allow claims, and does this correctly preserve reward accounting?
Does the lending market implementation handle sync_ledger reverts gracefully?
What is the total value at risk across all active markets?
validation_requirements: Test de-whitelist → re-whitelist cycle and verify claim correctness
Verify lending market behavior when sync_ledger reverts
Assess whether re-whitelisting creates accounting gaps

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Governance de-whitelists a market (accidentally or due to policy change). Users with pending CANTO rewards are immediately locked out. The lending market may also be unable to process withdrawals if it requires sync_ledger to succeed. Even re-whitelisting creates issues (TECH-H007: stale state, lastRewardBlock reset).
prerequisites: Governance de-whitelists a market with active users

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: All accrued unclaimed CANTO rewards for the market are permanently inaccessible
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users with unclaimed rewards in the de-whitelisted market
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Partial recovery via re-whitelisting but with accounting inconsistencies

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for user claim behavior after market de-whitelisting

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Immediately visible when users attempt to claim

threats.title: Retroactive Reward Rate Modification via setRewards for Un-Updated Markets
threats.hypothesis_description: The `setRewards()` function can set `cantoPerBlock[epoch]` for ANY epoch, including past epochs. Since `update_market()` uses lazy evaluation (only processes blocks when called), governance can retroactively change reward rates for past epochs that haven't been consumed yet by any market's update. This allows governance to arbitrarily increase or decrease rewards for specific markets after the fact.
threats.investigation_rationale: There is no check in setRewards that `epoch >= block.number`. Governance can call `setRewards(past_epoch, past_epoch, newRate)` to change the rate for a past epoch. If no market has been updated through that epoch yet, the new rate applies retroactively when update_market eventually processes those blocks.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setRewards only validates epoch alignment (`_fromEpoch % BLOCK_EPOCH == 0`), no check that epochs are in the future. Any epoch's rate can be overwritten.

preconditions: Governance calls setRewards for a past epoch
At least one whitelisted market has lastRewardBlock before the modified epoch
The market has not been updated through the modified epoch yet

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No temporal validation on epoch parameters
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: cantoPerBlock[pastEpoch] can be overwritten to any value. Markets with stale lastRewardBlock will use the new rate when eventually updated.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check `_fromEpoch >= currentEpoch`, no immutability for past epochs

analysis_questions: Can governance strategically modify past rates to favor specific markets?
How many markets typically have stale lastRewardBlock values?
Does the stated invariant 'total rewards per block should never exceed configured' hold with retroactive changes?
validation_requirements: Test setRewards for past epoch followed by update_market
Verify reward amounts change based on retroactive rate modification
Assess whether this violates the protocol's stated invariant

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Governance sets cantoPerBlock[epoch_100000] = 1e18 initially. At block 300000, governance modifies cantoPerBlock[epoch_100000] = 100e18. Market X, which hasn't been updated since block 50000, eventually gets updated. It receives 100x the originally intended rewards for epoch 100000, potentially draining the contract.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Arbitrary inflation or deflation of rewards for specific markets
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users in markets affected by retroactive rate changes
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Cannot undo once update_market processes the modified rates

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for retroactive rate modification impact

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring setRewards calls for past epochs
